pattern: Normalize a string to a canonical shape by replacing each character with the index of its first appearance; a word matches the pattern when both share the same canonical form.
hints: Two strings follow the same letter-to-letter mapping when every distinct letter in one maps to a unique distinct letter in the other — this is exactly what a bijection means.
Replace every character with a number that represents the order of its first appearance: 'abb' and 'mee' both become [0, 1, 1], revealing the match.
Keep a word only when its canonical encoding equals the pattern's canonical encoding; scan the word list in order and collect all that qualify.

edge_inputs.description: classic example from the problem — two words share the abb shape
single-character pattern matches every single-character word
pattern with all same letters — only words with all identical letters match
pattern with all distinct letters — only words with all distinct letters match
mapping must be bijective — word->pattern and pattern->word both consistent

stress.description: random words of length matching a random pattern; some crafted to match
